description: Canonical knowledge base for the architecture compiler. All extraction prompts, domain defaults, and language decisions draw from this file. Adding a new domain is a JSON edit, not a code change.

kernel.description: Talks to the kernel directly — syscalls, kqueue, FSEvents, ioctl, eBPF, drivers. Requires GC-free callback chains and C ABI compatibility.
kernel.rationale: Kernel callbacks require predictable latency. GC pauses in the callback path cause event loss. Rust's ownership model prevents use-after-free in callback chains.

network.description: Talks over the network — HTTP, gRPC, TCP, UDP, WebSocket, RPC.
network.rationale: Go's stdlib ships a production HTTP server. Goroutines map 1:1 to connections. Rust's hyper/tokio is competitive for latency-sensitive services.

filesystem.description: Reads/writes files, watches directories, manages paths.
filesystem.rationale: Filesystem operations are syscall-heavy. Rust and Go both have solid cross-platform FS abstractions.

human.description: Interacts with a human — TUI, CLI, dashboard, GUI.
human.rationale: Go's Bubble Tea for TUI. Swift for macOS-native. TypeScript for web dashboards.

database.description: Reads/writes to a database — SQL, KV store, document store.
database.rationale: Go's database/sql is the standard. Rust's diesel/sqlx for type-safe queries.

inter-process.description: Communicates with other processes on the same machine — pipes, shared memory, Unix sockets, signals.
inter-process.rationale: Unix domain sockets and pipes are POSIX primitives. Go's net package handles them natively.

request-parallel.description: Handles many independent operations at once. Each request/connection gets its own execution context.

pipeline.description: Data flows through sequential stages. Each stage can run concurrently. Backpressure between stages.

event-loop.description: Single-threaded with asynchronous I/O. One event at a time, no preemption.

fork-reap.description: Spawns child processes, waits for completion, checks exit codes.

none.description: Purely sequential. One thing at a time, start to finish.

stateless.description: Holds no state between operations. Pure input → output transformation.

in-flight.description: Holds temporary state while processing. State is bounded by request lifetime.

persistent.description: State must survive restarts. Written to disk or database.

shared-mutable.description: Multiple execution contexts read and write the same state simultaneously.

minutes.description: One-off. Runs, produces output, exits. Won't be maintained.

days.description: Prototype or experiment. Will be thrown away or rewritten.

months.description: Tool or utility. Used regularly, may evolve.

years.description: Daemon, service, or agent. Runs continuously. Must not leak, must handle edge cases, must have observability.

decades.description: Protocol, kernel module, or standard. Will outlive its original authors.

file-watcher.description: Watches filesystem changes and reacts to events. Kernel-adjacent. Callback chains. Must not drop events under load.
file-watcher.language_rationale: File watchers sit on kernel boundaries (kqueue/FSEvents/inotify). Rust's ownership model prevents use-after-free in callback chains. Go's GC pauses can cause event loss under high throughput. 14/17 OSS file watchers on GitHub use Rust.

oss_references.name: notify
oss_references.note: The standard Rust filesystem notification library

oss_references.name: fsnotify
oss_references.note: Go filesystem notifications. Simpler API, less performant under load.

common_failures: event loss under high throughput (buffer overflow)
callback after close (use-after-free)
race between event processing and shutdown
duplicate events from kernel coalescing
principles: Buffer events with backpressure — never block the kernel callback
Separate kernel thread from processing thread
Stop must be monotonic — once signalled, never un-signalled
Count events at every stage for observability

api-server.description: Serves HTTP/REST/gRPC endpoints. Handles concurrent requests with shared backend state.
api-server.language_rationale: Go's stdlib ships a production HTTP server. Goroutines map 1:1 to requests. Dominant in cloud infrastructure.

oss_references.name: Kubernetes apiserver
oss_references.note: Canonical Go API server at scale

oss_references.name: Prometheus
oss_references.note: Metrics server with concurrent scrape handling

common_failures: goroutine leak under connection exhaustion
unbounded request queues (OOM under load)
missing graceful drain (dropped in-flight requests)
shared state race between request handlers
principles: Every handler gets a timeout — never unbounded
Graceful shutdown: stop accepting → drain in-flight → close
Shared state behind a mutex or channel, never raw
Count requests, errors, and latency at the handler level

daemon.description: Background service or agent. Runs indefinitely. Managed by launchd/systemd.
daemon.language_rationale: Single static binary, no runtime. Ideal for LaunchAgents. The jw-* ecosystem standard.
common_failures: no signal handler — SIGTERM kills in-flight work
resource leak over weeks of uptime (goroutines, FDs, memory)
no health check — launchd can't tell if it's hung
principles: Handle SIGTERM gracefully — drain, flush, close
Emit metrics periodically even when idle (proof of life)
Use resource limits — ulimit for FDs, memory, CPU
Restart cleanly — no state corruption on crash

distributed-lock.description: Mutual exclusion across multiple processes or machines. Lease-based or token-based.
distributed-lock.language_rationale: Most lock services are Go (etcd, Chubby-style). Channels model lock acquisition naturally.

oss_references.name: etcd concurrency
oss_references.note: Distributed mutex and leader election via etcd

oss_references.name: Google Chubby
oss_references.note: Original distributed lock service paper (Burrows, OSDI 2006)

common_failures: split-brain: two processes think they hold the lock
lease expiry during GC pause (client loses lock, doesn't know)
fencing token not checked on write (stale holder corrupts data)
principles: Every lock operation carries a fencing token
Assume the lease can expire at any time — check before acting
Monotonic tokens — version numbers that only increase
Time-bounded, not infinite — leases expire, locks release

consensus.description: Agreement across a group of processes. Leader election, log replication, state machine replication.
consensus.language_rationale: Most consensus implementations are Go (etcd/raft, HashiCorp/raft). Channels match the RPC model.

oss_references.name: Hashicorp Raft
oss_references.note: Production RAFT used in Consul, Nomad

common_failures: split vote — no leader elected, system stalls
log divergence — followers fall behind, can't catch up
committed entries lost on restart (missing fsync)
membership change during leader election (joint consensus)
principles: Term numbers are monotonic — higher term always wins
Commit index advances only when quorum acks
Fsync before responding to client — committed means durable
Lockstep replication — followers apply same sequence as leader

event-sourcing.description: Append-only event log as source of truth. State is derived by replaying events.
event-sourcing.language_rationale: Event sourcing is I/O-bound. Go's concurrency handles replays efficiently. The jw-* ecosystem uses JSONL event sourcing.
common_failures: event ordering violation — events applied out of sequence
missing idempotency — duplicate events corrupt state
unbounded log growth — no compaction or retention policy
principles: Events are immutable — append only, never modify
Every event has a sequence number
State is a left-fold over events: foldl(apply, initial, events)
Idempotent application — applying the same event twice is safe

pipeline.description: Data flows through sequential stages. Each stage transforms and passes downstream.
pipeline.language_rationale: Go channels are pipeline stages. Select provides backpressure and cancellation natively.

oss_references.name: Go Concurrency Patterns
oss_references.note: Official Go blog on pipeline concurrency

common_failures: head-of-line blocking — slow consumer blocks entire pipeline
unbounded intermediate buffers — OOM under throughput spike
partial failure — one stage crashes, upstream keeps sending
cancellation doesn't propagate — goroutine leak
principles: Every inter-stage channel is bounded
Cancellation propagates forward AND backward
Each stage is independently monitored
Backpressure, not dropping — unless dropping is explicit policy

cli-tool.description: Command-line utility. Runs, does one thing, exits.
cli-tool.language_rationale: Single static binary, fast startup, no runtime. Go is the CLI default on this machine.
common_failures: no --help or usage output
no exit code differentiation (always 0 or always 1)
silent failure — error goes to stdout instead of stderr
principles: One thing well — do it and exit
Exit 0 for success, non-zero for failure, different codes for different failures
Errors to stderr, output to stdout
Flags, not positional arguments for anything non-trivial

general: Pick the weakest language that can do the job correctly — not the strongest.
Every component must cite its provenance (OSS reference, ledger record, or academic paper).
No pattern without evidence. If githits returns zero similar projects, flag for captain review.
The architecture is proven before any code is written. The implementation is verified, not trusted.
Every invariant has a runtime check. If the check fires, the proof was wrong — learn from it.
State must be explicit. Implicit state (globals, env vars, ambient context) is a bug waiting to happen.
Graceful degradation over silent failure. If something breaks, tell the operator what broke and what to do.
concurrency: Share memory by communicating, not communicate by sharing memory.
Every goroutine/task has a known exit path. No fire-and-forget without a timeout.
Bounded everything — queues, channels, pools, caches. Unbounded = eventual OOM.
Cancellation is part of the contract. Every async operation accepts a context or cancel token.
error_handling: Errors must tell you what to do next, not just what went wrong.
Don't log and return — return the error, let the caller decide.
Errors are values. Handle them, don't ignore them.
Panic only for unrecoverable programmer errors. Never for expected failure modes.
observability: Every long-running process emits health signals even when idle.
Counters over gauges — rates tell you more than point values.
The ledger is the audit trail. Every decision, every violation, every fix is recorded.
If you can't measure it, you can't prove it works.

ledger_learnings.description: Patterns learned from actual Bridge spawn history. Updated after each spawn cycle.
